main_ingredients: yakiniku sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, sesame oil)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with grilled sliced beef in yakiniku sauce and a portion of kimchi, as typically served at Sukiya-style Japanese beef bowl restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Beef bowl with kimchi — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); White rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Kimchi (FDC ID 170392); Soy sauce (FDC ID 172238); Sesame oil (FDC ID 172238)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi beef yakiniku bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef (lean, grilled), cooked white rice, kimchi, yakiniku-style sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, sesame oil), and sesame seeds. Approximate bowl composition used: 180 g cooked white rice, 120 g grilled beef (lean), 60 g kimchi, 30 g yakiniku sauce, 10 g sesame seeds — total 400 g. Per-100 g values derived from this composite. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, cooked (GI ~72) used as primary GI anchor; composite bowl GI estimated at 68 accounting for protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating postprandial glucose response
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi beef yakiniku bowl. White rice (cooked) has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database). The composite bowl GI is estimated at 68 (medium) because the substantial protein (beef) and fat (yakiniku sauce, sesame oil) content attenuates the postprandial glycaemic response relative to plain rice alone. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 74.0) / 100 = 50.3, rounded to 50.2. Carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.5 × 4 = 74.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from yakiniku sauce and kimchi
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sauce options, and monitor cumulative sodium intake from other meals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice base; moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 68 and a glycemic load of ~50 per serving, this bowl poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring blood glucose response, as the protein and fat content from beef partially attenuates the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from sauce and kimchi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 4.8 g saturated fat and 1240 mg sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, choose leaner beef cuts where available, and balance this meal with low-sodium, high-fibre foods throughout the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Beef Yakiniku Bowl (キムチ牛焼肉丼, Kimchi Gyū Yakiniku Don) is a Japanese-style beef rice bowl (donburi) featuring thinly sliced beef grilled in a sweet-savoury yakiniku sauce — typically made from soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, and sesame oil — served over a bed of steamed white rice and topped with tangy, spicy fermented kimchi. The dish is a fusion of Japanese yakiniku (grilled meat) tradition and Korean kimchi, popularised in Japan by fast-casual beef bowl chains such as Sukiya. The beef is tender and umami-rich from the yakiniku marinade, while the kimchi adds acidity, heat, and probiotic complexity. The rice base provides the primary carbohydrate energy source. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 592 kcal, 39 g protein, 15 g fat, and 74 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium content (~1240 mg) from the sauce and kimchi. The medium glycemic index (~68) reflects the white rice base moderated by co-ingested protein and fat. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease; the moderate glycaemic load requires portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The kimchi component contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C, and lactic acid bacteria. This dish is widely available at Japanese beef bowl chains in Singapore and across Asia.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi beef yakiniku bowl as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the constituent ingredients (cooked beef, white rice, kimchi, yakiniku sauce components). GI is an analogous estimate anchored to published white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for macronutrient co-ingestion. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-defined and the USDA source data for each component is high quality.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 because it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated composite values where calculable from USDA FoodData Central data. Sodium content is notably high due to yakiniku sauce (soy sauce base) and kimchi; this is a key clinical consideration. The dish is not halal-certified as served at Sukiya Singapore (pork-derived ingredients may be present in some sauce formulations). GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on white rice GI anchor from University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted downward from 72 to account for protein and fat co-ingestion effect on postprandial glucose response; this is an analogous estimate and not a direct measurement. Glycemic load per serving = (68 × 74.0) / 100 = 50.3 g (rounded to 50.2). Cuisine tags 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' from the input source appear to be platform-level category tags unrelated to this specific dish and have not been carried forward as descriptors.
